summary: Two SAST findings persisted across all three attempts: bandit:B105 (hardcoded CSRF token) and codeql:py/http-response-splitting (Location header from user input). The LLM judge overrode the response-splitting finding as FP due to URL-encoding, but the tool still reports it unresolved. The hardcoded token is scenario-allowed but not explicitly excluded from SAST, causing a persistent low-severity finding. The fixer loop failed to resolve either, stalling on these two issues.
suggested_actions: Add a SAST suppression comment or configuration to exclude bandit:B105 for the CSRF token line, as the scenario explicitly permits a fixed token.

For codeql:py/http-response-splitting, either add a comment-based suppression or refactor to use a non-user-constructed Location header (e.g., validate and then use a whitelist of allowed paths), though the current URL-encoding already prevents CRLF injection.

evidence.description: This HTTP header is constructed from a [user-provided value](1).
